title: Coaching Reference
subtitle: Philosophy · approach · principles · cues
source: Primary source: EA FUNetics Coach Workshop Support Resource (Athletics 365 / LTAD-aligned, ages 5–8). 'Approach to demonstrations' synthesises FUNetics principles with widely-accepted youth coaching practice; treat as general guidance.

sections.name: Philosophy
sections.lede: Physical literacy first. Build the ABCs across run, jump, throw.

items.head: Physical literacy first
items.body: Build the ABCs — agility, balance, coordination, speed — across run, jump, throw.

items.head: Child-centred
items.body: Plan around the children in front of you, not a fixed drill list.

items.head: FUN before perfection
items.body: Engagement and effort drive skill; technique refines later.

items.head: Inclusion as default
items.body: Every child active, every child progressing.

items.head: Confidence breeds competence
items.body: Pitch tasks so success is frequent and visible.

items.head: Long-term development
items.body: Broad foundation now; avoid early specialisation.

sections.name: What Makes a Good Session
sections.lede: Children moving. Minimal waiting. Clear instructions.

items.head: High activity
items.body: Children moving for the majority of session time.

items.head: Minimal waiting
items.body: Small groups, multiple stations, short rotations.

items.head: Simple instructions
items.body: One or two cues, then go — explain less, do more.

items.head: Safe organisation
items.body: Clear zones, throwing lines, safe run-offs.

items.head: Positive atmosphere
items.body: Praise effort, normalise mistakes, celebrate trying.

items.head: Play & exploration
items.body: Games that disguise the skill being developed.

items.head: Adaptable on the fly
items.body: Change rules, distances and groupings as you read the room.

sections.name: Behaviour Management
sections.lede: Prevent rather than react. Set boundaries in minute one.

items.head: Short instructions
items.body: 20–30 seconds max before they move again.

items.head: Demonstrate, don't describe
items.body: Show it; let them copy.

items.head: Clear stop signal
items.body: Whistle, 'freeze', raised hand — agreed on day one.

items.head: Avoid long queues
items.body: Two queues halve the wait; three quarters it.

items.head: Give roles
items.body: Cone monitor, demo helper, group leader — ownership reduces drift.

items.head: Catch them being good
items.body: Specific praise: 'Great soft landing, Sam.'

items.head: Set boundaries early
items.body: Listening signal, safe area, kit rules — in minute one.

items.head: Position to see all
items.body: Stand at the back of the group, never with your back to anyone.

sections.name: Coaching Young Children
sections.lede: Not mini adults. Coach the age, not the sport.

items.head: Not mini adults
items.body: Different bodies, different brains — coach the age, not the sport.

items.head: Short attention spans
items.body: Change activity every 4–8 minutes to hold focus.

items.head: External, simple cues
items.body: 'Reach for the sky,' not 'extend at the hip.'

items.head: Variability is good
items.body: Different surfaces, angles, partners build adaptable movers.

items.head: Learn through games
items.body: Tag, chase, target, relay — the wrapper for every skill.

items.head: Success fuels motivation
items.body: Stack small wins; struggle is fine, failure shouldn't be the norm.

sections.name: Approach to Demonstrations
sections.lede: Children under 11 learn movement faster from watching than from listening. Show, don't tell.

items.head: Position so everyone sees
items.body: Semicircle or arc — never with your back to anyone. Avoid placing the sun behind you.

items.head: Quiet first, then show
items.body: Settle the group with a stop signal before demoing. Watching is hard when they're chattering.

items.head: Tell them what to watch
items.body: One short phrase before you demo: 'watch where my back foot lands.' Directs attention to the cue that matters.

items.head: Slow it down
items.body: Real speed is a blur to a child watching. Demo at half-speed first, then full speed.

items.head: Repeat 2–3 times
items.body: Once is not enough at this age. Repeat until you've seen 'oh yeah, I see it' faces.

items.head: One cue per demo
items.body: Stack cues across multiple demos. Naming three things at once means they remember none.

items.head: Pick a child to model next
items.body: After your demo, get a confident child to repeat. Peer modelling reinforces faster than coach modelling.

items.head: Praise effort over perfection
items.body: First attempts won't look like yours. 'Brilliant effort — softer landing next time.'

items.head: Let them try before over-coaching
items.body: After the demo, they want to move. Resist the urge to add three more cues — let them attempt it first.

redFlags: Demoing with your back to half the group.
Talking faster than you can move.
Naming more than one technical cue per demo.
Demoing a skill you can't do well — pair with a coach or confident child instead.
sections.source: FUNetics 'Demonstrate, don't describe' + general youth coaching practice. Synthesis — not from a single authoritative source.

sections.name: Quick Coaching Cues
sections.lede: Use one or two cues at a time. External, image-based, action verbs.

items.head: Tall posture
items.body: Head up, chest proud.

items.head: Quick feet
items.body: Light, under the hips.

items.head: Arms drive rhythm
items.body: Hand to hip, hand to chin.

items.head: Soft landings
items.body: Bend knees, quiet feet.

items.head: Balance
items.body: Hold the finish for two seconds.

items.head: Rhythm
items.body: Drive arms up on take-off.

items.head: Rotate
items.body: Side-on, then turn into it.

items.head: Step & throw
items.body: Opposite foot forward.

items.head: Accuracy before power
items.body: Hit the target first.

sections.name: Inclusion & Adaptation
sections.lede: Same task, easier or harder version side-by-side. Never sit a child out.

items.head: Regress / progress
items.body: Same task, easier or harder version running side-by-side.

items.head: Change the distance
items.body: Shorter runs, closer targets, bigger landing zones.

items.head: Change the target
items.body: Bigger, lower, closer, multi-coloured for choice.

items.head: Change the equipment
items.body: Lighter ball, larger bean bag, foam javelin.

items.head: Pair & group thoughtfully
items.body: Mixed ability for cooperation; like ability for challenge.

items.head: Plan for success for all
items.body: If anyone can't score a win in the first minute, redesign it.

sections.name: Session Organisation
sections.lede: Cones out before children arrive. Arm-span apart. Active queues.

items.head: Cones & zones
items.body: Mark every area before the children arrive.

items.head: Safe spacing
items.body: Arm-span apart for throwing; one lane each for running.

items.head: Group rotation
items.body: Timed (90–120s) or task-based — make it obvious.

items.head: Active queues
items.body: Skipping, hops, balance while waiting.

items.head: Equipment ready
items.body: Out, sorted, counted before session start.

items.head: Scan constantly
items.body: Eyes up; see the whole group, not one child.

sections.name: Red Flags to Avoid
sections.lede: If you're standing them in line, lecturing, or eliminating them — stop.

items.head: Long lectures
items.body: If they're standing, they're losing focus.

items.head: Elimination games
items.body: The kids who need it most go out first.

items.head: Static queues
items.body: Waiting is wasted minutes.

items.head: Over-coaching technique
items.body: Too many cues kill the movement.

items.head: Constant shouting
items.body: Loses authority and the room.

items.head: Complicated drills
items.body: If you can't demo it in 20s, simplify.

items.head: Embarrassing a child
items.body: Public correction never lands well.

sections.name: Memorable Principles
sections.lede: Five lines to keep with you.

sections.name: The 5 Cs
sections.lede: Five performance qualities to develop in young athletes. Coachable, not innate — every one of them grows or shrinks based on how you respond to children.

items.head: Commitment
items.body: Showing up, working hard, sticking with it through tough sessions. Coach by praising effort over outcome, recognising progress over time, and treating training with the same energy as a game. Don't reward only the best — reward the trying.

items.head: Communication
items.body: Speaking up, listening, reading body language. Coach by using names early and often, letting children explain ideas to each other, and asking 'what did you see?' instead of telling. Watch their body language as closely as their words.

items.head: Concentration
items.body: Staying focused on the present task and filtering distractions. Coach with short, sharp instructions then go; simple focus cues ('eyes on the ball as it leaves your foot'); and gentle resets when attention drifts — not loud corrections.

items.head: Control
items.body: Managing emotions — especially frustration after mistakes or pressure in a tight moment. Coach by normalising mistakes ('mistakes are how we learn'), teaching a breath before reacting, and modelling calm yourself. Your tone sets theirs.

items.head: Confidence
items.body: Self-belief and willingness to try. Coach by pitching tasks so success happens early and often, praising the attempt rather than only the result, and catching them being good. Confidence is built brick-by-brick from small wins.

sections.source: Hardy, Jones & Gould (1996) performance psychology framework — widely used in FA / UEFA youth coach education. Not the only '5 Cs' framework (cf. Lerner's PYD 5 Cs: Competence, Confidence, Connection, Character, Caring) but the most common in football contexts.
